text: robotic pirostomal hernia repair utilizing TAR, a modified sugar baker technique. This is an eighty-three-year-old gentleman with large pirostomal hernia who is a former smoker with hypertension and a BMI of twenty-eight. The patient has significant surgical history as listed in this slide with over ten abdominal operations related to his rectal cancer. Lastly, the endoliosomy which was performed in two thousand seventeen.

This is an eighty-three-year-old gentleman with large pirostomal hernia who is a former smoker with hypertension and a BMI of twenty-eight. The patient has significant surgical history as listed in this slide with over ten abdominal operations related to his rectal cancer. Three trocars are placed for the robotic approach, positioned adjacent to the mucous fistula and maintaining a safe distance from the midline.
Three robotic trocars are placed adjacent to the mucous fistula, positioned with sufficient distance from the midline for the surgical approach.

The surgeon uses robotic instruments to perform a complex lysis of adhesions, employing sharp scissors and gentle retraction. After nearly two hours, the hernia contents are reduced, and the conduit is protected.

The surgeon begins the procedure with complex lysis of adhesions using robotic instruments, including sharp scissors and gentle retraction. This is followed by the reduction of hernia contents, ensuring the conduit is protected during the process.

The surgeon enters the retrorectus space by incising the posterior rectus sheath approximately one centimeter from the linea alba. Dissection proceeds laterally towards the lateral edge of the rectus sheath and linea semilunaris, caudally towards the pubic tubercle, and cephalically towards the subcostal space. The area around the ostomy site is dissected to free adhesions and connections.

The surgeon enters the retrorectus space by incising the posterior rectus sheath approximately one centimeter from the linea alba. Dissection proceeds laterally towards the lateral edge of the rectus sheath and linea semilunaris, caudally towards the pubic tubercle, and cephalically towards the subcostal space, carefully dissecting around the ostomy site to free adhesions.

The surgeon approximates tears in the posterior fascia using visible sutures, completing a flap of at least thirty centimeters in length. Component separation is initiated from both sides, with visible division of muscle fibers and lateral mobilization on the patient's right side to create adequate space for mesh overlap. The fibers of the diaphragm are visible during the procedure.

The surgeon approximates tears in the posterior fascia using sutures, completing a flap of at least thirty centimeters in length. Component separation is initiated from both sides, with visible division of muscle fibers and lateral mobilization on the patient's right side to create space for mesh overlap. The fibers of the diaphragm are visible during the procedure.

The surgeon visually identifies the prior repair of an inguinal hernia. The dissection progresses laterally, completing the mobilization of the conduit. The hernia sac is dissected, and the posterior fascia is closed after the conduit is lateralized.

The video shows the prior repair of an inguinal hernia. The surgeon mobilizes further laterally, performing lateralization of the conduit. The dissection of the hernia sac is completed, and the posterior fascia is closed after the lateralization of the conduit.

The surgeon uses a 2-0 absorbable barbed suture, measuring twelve inches, to close the posterior fascia. Following this, the conduit is secured with PEXI, and the defect is closed using a running 2-0 absorbable barbed suture.

The surgeon uses a 2-0 absorbable barbed suture, approximately twelve inches in length, to close the posterior fascia. Following this, a PEXI of the conduit is performed, and the defect is closed with a running 2-0 absorbable barbed suture.

The surgeon measures the defect with a ruler in the retrorectus space and blocks the suture by going backward. Observable sutures are used to pexy the conduit on each side. All tears are addressed to prevent future herniation of intraperitoneal structures into the retrorectus space. A 30x20 microporous medium weight polypropylene mesh is visible.

In the retrorectus space, the surgeon measures the defect with a ruler and secures the conduit on each side using absorbable sutures. All tears are addressed to prevent future herniation of intraperitoneal structures. A 30x20 cm microporous medium weight polypropylene mesh is placed.

The surgeon creates a slit in the mesh to accommodate the conduit, followed by loose fixation of the mesh in cephalad, lateral, and inferior directions. The retrorectus space is then closed by suturing the fascia with barbed sutures.

The surgeon performs pexy of the conduit to the peritoneum and posterior fascia on both sides, securing these structures in place.
